title: UK Social Media Ban, SpaceX Orbital Data Centers, F-35 Readiness Crisis
subhead: Britain announces the world's strictest social media restrictions for children, while SpaceX reveals plans for a million-satellite AI megaconstellation and the Pentagon seeks $13.7B to fix F-35 readiness rates that have fallen to 25%.

sections.heading: UK Bans Social Media for Under-16s
sections.body:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced what may be the world's strictest social media restrictions for children, banning all social media access for users under 16 beginning in spring 2027. The ban covers TikTok, Instagram, Facebook, X, Snapchat, and YouTube, though messaging apps like WhatsApp and Signal are exempt.

The UK measures go further than Australia's landmark legislation by also prohibiting livestreaming and stranger contact features for under-16s, with additional protections enabled by default for 16- and 17-year-olds. The government is also considering overnight social media curfews for all minors.

The policy follows a public consultation that drew over 100,000 submissions, with more than 90% of supporting parents backing an outright ban. Critics argue the restrictions will simply push teenagers toward less regulated platforms and VPNs, citing Australian data showing continued social media use despite that country's ban.

Meta and Snapchat pushed back immediately, with Meta arguing that bans risk isolating teenagers from online communities and YouTube emphasizing its 'curated, supervised, beneficial experiences.' The announcement comes as Starmer faces political pressure after disappointing local election results.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK is now the second major Western nation to impose a blanket social media ban for minors, but with significantly broader scope than Australia's approach. The policy marks a sharp escalation in government regulation of Big Tech and could reshape how platforms design youth experiences globally.

sections.heading: SpaceX Reveals Orbital AI Data Center Ambitions
sections.body:
SpaceX has filed plans with the FCC for up to one million 'orbital data center' satellites, revealing its first AI-dedicated satellite — dubbed AI1 — in a video posted June 9. The move positions SpaceX to build computing infrastructure in orbit, with Musk stating the satellite is 'much simpler than a Starlink satellite.'

The initiative marks SpaceX's entry into the orbital computing market, where companies like Muon Space are already developing Starship-class satellite platforms for data centers. Muon Space announced its platform June 3 with an initial launch slated for 2028.

Orbital data centers could potentially bypass some terrestrial infrastructure constraints, offering advantages for latency-critical applications and secure computing. SpaceX is planning orbital AI computing tests by the end of next year, according to industry sources.

The filing comes as SpaceX prepares for its highly anticipated IPO, with valuation dependent in part on Starship development and the orbital AI data center business.
sections.whyItMatters: SpaceX's plan represents an unprecedented scale for satellite infrastructure — dwarfing current Starlink by orders of magnitude. If realized, it would create a new category of computing sovereignty, with nations potentially dependent on U.S.-controlled orbital infrastructure for AI processing.

sections.heading: F-35 Readiness Crisis Deepens Despite $13.7B Investment
sections.body:
A Government Accountability Office report reveals the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter's full mission capable rate has fallen to just 25% through fiscal 2025, down from 38% in previous years. The mission capable rate (ability to perform at least one tasked mission) declined from 67% to 44%.

The Pentagon plans to spend an additional $13.7 billion through 2031 to boost sustainment — $8 billion for the Air Force, $3.2 billion for the Navy, and $2.6 billion for the Marines. But the GAO warns that 'constrained industry capacity to meet increasing demand for parts could threaten its success.'

The GAO also found that 'hundreds of millions' in contract incentives paid to contractors since 2020 to improve readiness 'failed to incentivize contractors to achieve air vehicle readiness requirements.' The Defense Department currently operates over 800 F-35s and plans to buy 1,700 more by the mid-2040s.

The fighter has been deployed during the recent Iran conflict, underscoring operational concerns amid declining availability.
sections.whyItMatters: With only one in four F-35s fully mission capable, the Pentagon's most expensive weapons program faces a readiness crisis even as the U.S. military relies on it for air superiority across multiple theaters. The $13.7 billion fix may not succeed if parts supply chains remain constrained.

sections.heading: Russia Builds Arctic Infrastructure for Major Troop Deployments
sections.body:
Russia is constructing facilities for tens of thousands of troops along NATO's northern flank, according to analysis from The War Zone. The buildout includes new bases and infrastructure near the Arctic, prompting NATO to bolster its own defenses with a new Arctic combat force.

The expansion signals Moscow's intent to maintain permanent military pressure on NATO's Nordic members, particularly Finland and Norway, even as its main forces remain committed in Ukraine. The construction includes housing, storage, and logistics facilities sized for division-level deployments.

NATO allies have been working to strengthen Arctic defenses, with the UK's foreign minister calling for increased alliance focus on the region during visits to Finland and Norway earlier this year.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's Arctic buildout creates a persistent threat on NATO's northern flank that will require sustained allied resources to counter, even after any Ukraine settlement. The infrastructure investment suggests Moscow is planning for long-term confrontation.

sections.heading: FCAS Fighter Program Collapses, Germany and Spain Form New Coalition
sections.body:
Germany and France have scrapped their flagship Future Combat Air System fighter jet program after years of disputes over requirements and industrial workshare. The $115 billion effort was intended to produce Europe's premier sixth-generation combat aircraft.

Within days of the FCAS collapse, Germany and Spain announced 'Team Gen 6' — a new coalition to develop sixth-generation fighter technology. The partnership could reshape Europe's future fighter landscape but still requires political backing, funding, and likely additional partners.

The FCAS collapse deals a blow to European defense cooperation and leaves France potentially pursuing its own separate sixth-generation program. Spanish industry representatives are already calling for inclusion in any new European fighter project.

The program's failure after nearly a decade of planning raises questions about Europe's ability to coordinate on major defense programs amid increasing threats from Russia and China.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's inability to sustain its flagship fighter program weakens the continent's defense industrial base at a critical moment. The split could lead to fragmented national programs that duplicate costs and reduce interoperability.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals Tensions Surface at G7
sections.body:
Japan is pushing G7 leaders to create a coordinated critical minerals stockpile system to reduce dependence on China, which controls 90% of rare-earth processing. The proposal comes as the Trump administration's plan for a Western critical minerals trading bloc with government-supported pricing faces resistance from allies.

U.S. business groups report that some critical minerals from China are now 'nearly unobtainable' due to export controls and licensing delays, with three-quarters of impacted companies searching for alternative suppliers. A $204 million U.S. investment in France aims to expand rare-earth metals and magnet manufacturing operations by 2030.

China has tightened controls on rare earth and other strategic materials, with Beijing planning a policy briefing next month for metals firms on export regulations. The moves create a bifurcated critical minerals market amid intensifying U.S.-China tensions.
sections.whyItMatters: Critical minerals supply chains are becoming a front line in U.S.-China competition. Japan's stockpile proposal and U.S. investment in French processing reflect growing Western recognition that China's dominance poses strategic risks.

sections.heading: Taiwan Fires HIMARS Toward China in First Strait Drill
sections.body:
Taiwan conducted its first live-fire exercise using U.S.-supplied HIMARS rocket launchers from its western coastline facing China, signaling enhanced defensive capabilities against potential invasion. The M142 High Mobility Artillery Rocket System demonstrated 'shoot-and-scoot' techniques for mobile defense.

The drill highlights Taipei's focus on mobile defense capabilities as China continues military pressure on the island. The HIMARS system has proven effective in Ukraine and represents a significant upgrade to Taiwan's deterrent posture.

The exercise comes amid continued Chinese military activity near Taiwan and follows U.S. arms sales that have strengthened the island's defenses. Taiwan has also tested missiles on its China-facing coast to demonstrate defensive readiness.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's public use of HIMARS in the Taiwan Strait sends a clear signal to Beijing about enhanced defensive capabilities and continued U.S. military support. The exercise demonstrates interoperability with American weapons systems critical to Taiwan's defense strategy.

bottomLine: Today's developments reveal three distinct technology-power trajectories: democratic governments asserting unprecedented control over digital platforms (UK social media ban), private companies building sovereign orbital infrastructure at unprecedented scale (SpaceX), and Western defense programs struggling with readiness and coordination despite massive investment (F-35, FCAS). The underlying pattern is one of technological capability outpacing institutional capacity — whether in regulating platforms, managing defense supply chains, or sustaining multinational cooperation.
sources: CNBC, WIRED, Reuters, GAO, The War Zone, Defense News, International Business Times, SpaceNews, Stripes, Associated Press, Yahoo Finance, MSN, Breaking Defense, Anadolu Agency
